title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: Six paragraphs have been removed from the text. Choose the correct paragraph (A-G) to fill each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.title: The Last Word: Saving Endangered Languages
content.text: Every two weeks, a language dies. When the last speaker of a tongue passes away, we lose more than just a set of words; we lose a unique way of seeing the world. Languages are repositories of traditional knowledge, containing centuries of wisdom about local ecosystems, medicinal plants, and oral histories. || (41) || [41] Today, of the roughly 7,000 languages spoken worldwide, nearly half are considered endangered [/41]. Most of these are indigenous tongues, pushed to the margins by the dominance of global languages like English, Spanish, and Mandarin. The pressure to assimilate into the global economy often forces younger generations to abandon their ancestral speech. || (42) || [42] However, a new wave of 'digital activism' is beginning to turn the tide [/42]. Instead of seeing technology as a threat, indigenous communities are using it as a tool for revitalization. From mobile apps that teach vocabulary to social media groups where elders record traditional stories, the internet is providing a new 'virtual territory' for these languages. || (43) || [43] In Australia, for example, the Gamilaraay people have developed a dictionary app [/43]. This allows young people to learn their language on the same devices they use for gaming and music, making the learning process feel modern and relevant rather than a chore of the past. || (44) || [44] But technology alone is not a silver bullet [/44]. For a language to truly survive, it needs to be spoken in the home and the community, not just stored on a server. It needs to be a living, breathing part of daily life, used in the grocery store as much as in the classroom. || (45) || [45] This is where 'language nests' come in [/45]. This model, first pioneered in New Zealand by the Māori, involves immersing young children in their ancestral tongue from infancy. By surrounding them with fluent elders in a natural, play-based environment, the language is passed down organically. || (46) || [46] The success of these programs has been remarkable [/46]. Not only are children becoming fluent in Māori, but they are also showing improved academic performance and a stronger sense of cultural identity. It turns out that preserving a language isn't just about the past; it's about giving future generations a sense of belonging in a rapidly changing world.

paragraphs.B: [42] This loss of linguistic diversity is often compared to the loss of biodiversity [/42]. Just as an ecosystem is healthier when it has many species, the human experience is richer when it has many ways to express itself.
paragraphs.G: [41] It is estimated that by the end of this century, 90% of the world's languages could be extinct or moribund [/41]. This is a cultural catastrophe that many compare to the burning of a library in every village.
paragraphs.D: [44] There is also the challenge of 'orthography'—or how to write a language that has only ever been spoken [/44]. Creating a written system for a purely oral tongue can be a source of intense debate within a community.
paragraphs.A: [43] Similar projects are popping up from the Amazon to the Arctic [/43]. These digital tools are helping to bridge the gap between the elders, who hold the knowledge, and the youth, who hold the technology.
paragraphs.C: [46] This approach has since been adopted by indigenous groups worldwide [/46]. From the Hawaiian 'Pūnana Leo' to the 'immersion schools' of the Navajo in the US, the nest model is proving that extinction is not inevitable.
paragraphs.F: [45] Such initiatives recognize that the heart of a language is social connection [/45]. They focus on creating 'safe spaces' where speakers can interact without the fear of being judged or the pressure to switch to a dominant tongue.
paragraphs.E: Some governments have tried to ban indigenous languages in schools in an effort to promote national unity. This has often resulted in the permanent loss of unique dialects and traditions.

questions.explanation: G aporta la estadística catastrófica (90% de extinción) que refuerza el punto de que casi la mitad de las lenguas están en peligro.
B compara la pérdida lingüística con la biodiversidad, siguiendo la idea de que perdemos una forma única de ver el mundo.
A menciona que proyectos similares (al de Australia) están surgiendo en otras partes, conectando con el uso de herramientas digitales.
D añade el desafío técnico de la escritura (ortografía), lo cual matiza que la tecnología no es una solución mágica (silver bullet).
F explica el concepto de 'safe spaces' y conexión social, que es la base de los 'language nests' mencionados después.
C confirma que el modelo de 'nidos' se ha adoptado en todo el mundo, demostrando que la extinción no es inevitable.
